title: Boat and Marine Detailing Business
metaTitle: How to Start a Boat Detailing Business — Real Costs, Earnings, and Steps
metaDescription: Honest guide to starting a boat and marine detailing business: real startup costs, realistic per-foot pricing and earnings, marina access, and what most people get wrong.
bestFor: Detail-oriented people who like hands-on outdoor work, live near water, and want higher per-job tickets than car detailing
biggestRisk: Damaging expensive gelcoat, electronics, or upholstery on a high-value boat and facing a claim that dwarfs the job price
whatItIs: A boat and marine detailing business cleans, restores, and protects boats — washing hulls, compounding and polishing oxidized gelcoat, waxing or applying ceramic coatings, cleaning and conditioning vinyl and upholstery, polishing stainless and chrome, and clearing mold and salt from decks and cabins. It overlaps with car detailing only superficially: boats use gelcoat rather than automotive clearcoat, they sit in harsh sun and saltwater, and the surfaces, oxidation, and access challenges are different enough that boat owners pay a meaningful premium for someone who genuinely knows marine work.
dailyReality: Most days you drive to a marina, boatyard, dry-stack facility, or a customer's driveway where a trailered boat sits, then spend three to eight hours per vessel on your feet, often kneeling, reaching, and working in direct sun and humidity. A simple wash-and-wax might take a few hours; a full oxidation correction and ceramic coat on a 30-foot boat can run one to two days. You handle a buffer for hours at a time, manage water and runoff at the dock, and protect electronics and canvas from spray. Around the work, you spend 30 to 60 minutes most days quoting by length, coordinating marina access and gate codes, scheduling around weather and the owner's use of the boat, and collecting payment.

startupCostsItemized.item: Compounds, polishes, marine wax, ceramic coating, pads
Marine-safe soaps, vinyl/upholstery cleaners, mold and oxidation removers
Wet/dry vacuum, extractor, brushes, microfiber, hoses
Portable water tank, pump, or 12V supply for no-spigot docks
Reliable vehicle storage/shelving, generator, or work van fit-out
Google Business Profile, simple website, before/after photos

realEarnings.yearOne: Beginners working part-time around a job typically earn $2,000 to $4,500 per month during the boating season, completing one to three boats a week. Going full-time in season, a solo operator who books steadily usually reaches $4,000 to $8,000 per month once word spreads at one or two marinas.
realEarnings.experienced: Operators with two or more seasons, strong reviews, and standing relationships at multiple marinas commonly report $7,000 to $15,000 per month in peak season, helped by recurring wash contracts and high-margin ceramic coating jobs. Income dips sharply or stops in winter in cold-climate regions, so annual figures are lower than peak months suggest.
realEarnings.topEarners: Top solo and small-crew operations in year-round boating markets (Florida, the Gulf, Southern California) gross $150,000 to $300,000+ annually, but reaching that takes multiple seasons, a helper or two, ceramic coating expertise, exclusive or preferred-vendor status at busy marinas, and a shift toward managing schedules and crews rather than buffing every hull yourself.
realEarnings.whatAffectsMost: Climate and season length matter enormously — a year-round market can earn two to three times what a four-month northern season allows. After that, pricing discipline, selling high-ticket oxidation correction and ceramic coatings, and locking in recurring monthly wash accounts move income far more than equipment.
realEarnings.perHour: Effective rates run roughly $60 to $150 per hour of actual work for solo operators, higher on ceramic and correction jobs and lower on basic washes. Counting driving, marina access waits, quoting, and weather cancellations, realistic blended rates are often $45 to $100 per hour.

howToStart: Week 1: Practice compounding, polishing, and waxing on your own or a friend's boat until gelcoat results are consistent, and learn the difference between automotive and marine products. Get general liability insurance with care-custody-control coverage before touching a paying customer's boat — a single buffer burn on gelcoat can cost more than the job.

Week 2: Set per-foot pricing (the marine standard) for wash, wax, oxidation removal, and ceramic, plus add-ons for interiors and bottoms. Build a Google Business Profile with sharp before/after photos and visit local marinas, boatyards, and dry-stack facilities to ask about access rules and posting a card on the board.

Month 1: Complete your first handful of paid boats, track real time by length and condition so your per-foot pricing actually profits, and ask every happy owner for a Google review and a referral to the next slip over. Confirm runoff and washdown rules at each facility so you do not get banned.

Days 30-90: Pursue preferred-vendor or referral status with one or two marinas and dealers, introduce recurring monthly wash-down accounts for steady cash, and decide whether to invest in ceramic coating training and a portable water setup based on the jobs you are actually winning.

mustHave: Physical stamina for hours of buffing, kneeling, and reaching in heat and sun
Care and attention to detail around very expensive, easily damaged surfaces and electronics
Reliability and professionalism owners will trust with a boat worth far more than your van
canLearn: Correct compound, pad, and speed choices to correct gelcoat oxidation without burning it
Ceramic coating application and surface prep (worth a paid course or hands-on mentoring)
Per-foot pricing and how condition and access change your real hourly rate
separators: Mastering oxidation correction and ceramic coatings, the high-ticket services that drive margin
Building trusted relationships and preferred-vendor status at busy marinas so jobs come to you
Knowing gelcoat, canvas, and electronics well enough to avoid the damage claims that erase a season

whatPeopleGetWrong: Treating boats like big cars — using automotive products and pressure that streak, etch, or fail on gelcoat and marine canvas
Burning through gelcoat with a rotary buffer at the wrong speed, turning a polish job into an expensive repair
Skipping care-custody-control insurance, then facing a claim on a $100,000 boat from one scratch or water-damaged electronic
Underpricing oxidation correction and ceramic work, which are labor-intensive and where the real money is
Ignoring each marina's washdown, runoff, and access rules and getting banned from the best customer pools
Building a business around a short season with no winterizing, shrink-wrap, or off-season service to fill the gap

toolsAndEquipment.note: The core of correction and ceramic prep. A controllable rotary is essential for serious oxidation but dangerous on gelcoat without practice.

toolsAndEquipment.item: Marine compounds, polishes, wax, and ceramic coating
toolsAndEquipment.note: Use marine-specific products; automotive formulas do not hold up to sun and salt.

toolsAndEquipment.item: Vinyl/upholstery cleaners, mold and oxidation removers
toolsAndEquipment.note: Interiors and non-skid decks are a big part of the job; buy marine-safe chemistry.
For bilges, cabins, carpets, and upholstery. Extractors pay off once you do interiors.

toolsAndEquipment.item: Portable water tank, pump, or 12V supply
toolsAndEquipment.note: Many slips and dry-stacks have no convenient spigot; bring your own water once you scale.

toolsAndEquipment.item: Microfiber, brushes, pads, ladders, extension tools
toolsAndEquipment.note: Boats are tall and awkward; safe reach and the right pads matter.

methods: Building relationships with marinas, boatyards, dry-stack facilities, and dealers for referrals and preferred-vendor status
A complete Google Business Profile with marine before/after photos and steady reviews
Boat owner Facebook groups, marina bulletin boards, and local yacht and boating clubs
Walking the docks and slips with cards while you are already on site for a job
Recurring monthly wash-down accounts that turn one boat owner into year-round income and referrals to neighboring slips
howToFindCustomers.whereCustomersAre: Boat owners cluster at marinas, yacht clubs, dry-stack storage, and boat ramps, plus trailer-boat owners at home in coastal and lake communities. Dealers and brokers also need boats detailed for sale and after delivery.
howToFindCustomers.timeToClientBase: Once you have access to a busy marina, jobs can come quickly through dock referrals, often within the first few weeks. A reliable, season-filling client base usually takes one to two full boating seasons of consistent work and reviews.
howToFindCustomers.wasteOfTime: Broad paid ads and generic flyers far from the water rarely reach boat owners. Early on, marina relationships, dock referrals, and a few stunning before/after photos convert far better than any advertising spend.

howItScales.toFullTime: Yes in a long-season or year-round market; harder where the season is only four to six months. Solo operators can reach a full-time seasonal income within a year or two, but cold-climate operators usually add winterizing, shrink-wrap, or a complementary service to bridge winter.
howItScales.hireAndStepBack: Possible but demanding. Hiring lets you cover more boats and bigger jobs, but buffing gelcoat is high-skill, high-risk work, so a damaging hire can cost more than they earn. Stepping back requires trained techs, tight quality control, and standing marina relationships that survive your absence.
howItScales.sellable: A detailing business with recurring wash accounts, marina relationships, trained staff, and a brand can sell for a modest multiple of profit. A pure solo operation built entirely on the owner's hands and reputation is much harder to transfer.
howItScales.whatScalingRequires: Standardized per-foot pricing and process, redundant equipment, hired and trained techs, preferred status at multiple facilities, and systems that book and route jobs without your personal time. Seasonality and the skill ceiling on correction work are the main constraints.

strongFit: You live near year-round or long-season boating water and are comfortable around marinas
You are detail-oriented, patient, and careful around expensive, fragile surfaces
You are physically fit and prefer hands-on outdoor work to a desk
You are comfortable selling higher-ticket correction and ceramic jobs, not just cheap washes
poorFit: You want passive income or to avoid physical, weather-exposed labor
You live in a short-season cold climate with no plan to fill the off-season
You are careless with equipment or anxious about working on high-value property
You are unwilling to carry proper care-custody-control insurance or learn marine-specific technique
askYourself: Is my market long enough in season, or do I have a real plan for winter income?
Am I willing to invest the practice and possibly paid training to safely correct gelcoat and apply ceramics?
Can I get access and build trust at the marinas and yards where the boat owners actually are?

faqs.question: How is boat detailing different from car detailing?
faqs.answer: Boats use gelcoat rather than automotive clearcoat, sit in harsh sun and saltwater, and have canvas, vinyl, non-skid decks, and electronics that need marine-specific products and technique. Oxidation correction on gelcoat is more involved and riskier than polishing a car, and access at docks is harder. Because of all this, boat owners pay a meaningful premium over car detailing rates.

faqs.question: How do you price boat detailing?
faqs.answer: The marine standard is per linear foot of boat length, with separate rates for wash-and-wax, oxidation correction, ceramic coating, interiors, and bottom cleaning. Rates commonly run from a few dollars per foot for a basic wash to $20-$40+ per foot for full correction or ceramic, adjusted for condition and access. Track your real hours by length so your per-foot pricing actually profits.

faqs.question: Do I need a special license to detail boats?
faqs.answer: No specific detailing license is required in most areas, but you need a business registration and general liability insurance, ideally with care-custody-control coverage given how valuable boats are. Marinas and boatyards often require proof of insurance before letting you work on their property, and you must follow their washdown and runoff rules.

faqs.question: Is boat detailing seasonal?
faqs.answer: Very much so outside year-round markets. In cold climates the active season may run only four to six months, with a busy spring launch and fall haul-out rush. Many operators add winterizing, shrink-wrapping, or storage prep to fill winter, while operators in Florida, the Gulf, and Southern California can work nearly year-round.

faqs.question: Do I need my own water and power at the dock?
faqs.answer: Often yes. Many slips, mooring fields, and dry-stack facilities lack a convenient spigot or outlet, so a portable water tank, pump, or 12V supply lets you work where others cannot. You can start by using available dock water and a generator, and add a self-contained setup as you take on harder-access jobs.

faqs.question: How dangerous is it to buff gelcoat?
faqs.answer: Riskier than buffing a car. Too much heat or speed from a rotary can burn through gelcoat, exposing the underlying layer and turning a polish into a costly repair. This is why practice, the right pads and speeds, and care-custody-control insurance matter before you take on correction work on valuable boats.

faqs.question: How quickly can I make money detailing boats?
faqs.answer: If you can get access to a busy marina, your first paid boats can come within a few weeks through dock referrals. Building a season-filling, reliable client base usually takes one to two full boating seasons of consistent quality work and reviews.

dataSources: U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics — Cleaning and Maintenance occupations and self-employed services data
